text: The surgeon performs adhesiolysis on complex adhesions using traction and counter-traction techniques. Initially, flimsy adhesions are divided to create working space. The omentum is carefully separated from the bowel, which is adhered to the abdominal wall. A 30-degree camera through the Palmar's point port provides a view of the adhesions for precise division.

The surgeon carefully pulls down the bowel adherent to the anterior abdominal wall, using a closed grasper to apply traction without directly holding the bowel. Sharp division is performed on the flimsy adhesions, creating more space and facilitating surgical progress. The surgeon continues to identify and divide the easier adhesion areas before addressing the tougher regions, using traction, counter-traction, and sharp division techniques.

The surgeon identifies the incision site at the previous ileostomy closure where the bowel has herniated. The herniated bowel loop is gradually drawn down from either side, using traction and counter-traction techniques. The hernia sac is divided, allowing reduction of the herniated bowel. A lupithalon suture is passed through the skin into the abdomen and used to close the hernia defect in a continuous fashion, then tied outside the entry site.

A 15x15 cm composite mesh is placed inside the abdominal cavity for hernia repair. Upper and lower transfixation sutures are applied, followed by multiple 2-0 PDS sutures to secure the mesh, ensuring no gaps are left to prevent bowel herniation. The omentum is repositioned over the small bowel.
